nativeRange.habitat: Sand banks and shallows of major rivers, slow-moving tributaries with mud or sand substrates, and flooded forest margins during the wet season.

decorPreferences: Deep fine sand substrate (avoid sharp gravel that abrades the disc)
Large open footprint with minimal obstructions
Smooth rounded rockwork or driftwood placed against the glass, not loose mid-tank
Tight-fitting lid (rays may breach the surface)

diet.notes: Tends to take meaty foods such as frozen bloodworms, tubifex, mysis, krill, mussels, cockles, prawns, squid, whitebait, and earthworms. Generally advised to feed twice daily and to avoid mammalian meats and live feeder fish (disease/nutrition risk).

goodWith: Large peaceful upper-water dwellers (silver arowana, severums, geophagine cichlids)
avoidWith: Discus and other delicate soft-water species
Territorial loricariids (except Hypostomus) that may rasp the disc

imageCandidates.notes: Steven G. Johnson, 2707x1802. Sharp, vibrant side view of a live specimen showing the whole disc plus tail with textbook peacock-eye ocellated spots (yellow centres ringed in black on a brown disc).
Raimond Spekking, 1500x805. Top-down view of two live individuals over sand, clearly displaying the diagnostic ocellated (peacock-eye) pattern; minor chromatic fringing on the tails.
Karelj, 2600x1582. Crisp, well-lit live specimen with full disc and tail, strong orange-ringed ocelli; public-domain alternative angle.

summary: The Freshwater Stingray (Potamotrygon motoro) is a large, bottom-dwelling ray from South American rivers. Adults typically reach 35–50 cm across the disc, carry a venomous tail spine, and require very large, heavily filtered, warm soft-water aquariums.
careNotes:
This entry focuses on Potamotrygon motoro, the Ocellate River Stingray, as the species most commonly offered in the aquarium trade under the 'Freshwater Stingray' name. Other Potamotrygon species appear in the hobby with meaningfully different care requirements: P. leopoldi (the Black Diamond or Polka Dot Stingray, around 40–45 cm across the disc, a blackwater specialist), P. henlei (the Bigtooth River Stingray, around 50 cm, similar care to leopoldi), and P. jabuti (the Pearl Stingray, around 40 cm, generally considered somewhat less aggressive) all sit at the higher end of difficulty and price. Anyone purchasing a species other than P. motoro should research that species specifically before acquiring.

Adult disc width in well-kept aquaria typically falls between 35 and 50 cm, and the tank footprint required to accommodate an animal this size — combined with its substantial waste output and characteristic gliding swimming pattern — makes tank size the most critical constraint in this species' care. A tank at least 240 cm long and holding 2000 litres or more is strongly advised; under-sized housing is widely cited as a leading cause of captive ray failures, and larger is always preferable.

Water quality: Freshwater stingrays are extremely sensitive to ammonia, nitrite, and elevated nitrate. Target values are 0 ppm ammonia and nitrite, with nitrate kept below 20 ppm (lower is better). Weekly water changes of 40–50% are typical practice, and filtration should be sized well above what the tank volume alone would suggest.

SAFETY: The Freshwater Stingray possesses a venomous caudal spine (a barbed tail spine that delivers a protein-based venom capable of causing intense pain and tissue degeneration). The spine is periodically shed and replaced by a new one, so a ray without a visible spine is not necessarily unarmed. Puncture-resistant gloves covering the arm should be worn for any in-tank maintenance. Rays should never be netted — always move them using solid containers — and the working end of the tail should be monitored at all times. Households with young children, immunocompromised members, or limited time for careful maintenance should weigh these risks seriously before acquiring the species.

LEGAL STATUS — VERIFY BEFORE PURCHASE: Potamotrygon species are banned outright for private possession in at least Arizona, Arkansas, California, Georgia, Mississippi, Nevada, Oklahoma, Texas, and Utah; Hawaii prohibits all stingrays; Colorado and Florida require permits. Brazilian export of wild-caught specimens is also tightly regulated. Laws change and local ordinances may impose additional restrictions — confirm the current rules in your jurisdiction before acquiring any freshwater stingray.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been achieved by specialist keepers. The Freshwater Stingray reproduces by matrotrophic viviparity — a form of live-bearing in which the developing young receive nutrients directly from the mother rather than from a yolk sac alone. Gestation is generally reported at around 9–12 weeks, with litters typically ranging from 1 to 8 pups. Males have been reported as aggressive toward females during the breeding period, so pair selection and close observation are important. Consistently stable water parameters and weekly water changes of around 50% are commonly recommended throughout the breeding process. Captive breeding is not considered a realistic project for keepers new to the genus.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Promoted to primary because the slice's nominal primary (Aquarium Co-Op) does not publish a freshwater-stingray care page. SeriouslyFish provided sizing, water parameters, diet, temperament, breeding mode, and venom advisory.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Potamotrygonidae / Myliobatiformes), max disc width 50 cm WD, and South American native range. FishBase lists pH 5.0-6.0 (wild), narrower than hobby targets.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Slice primary source per source-urls.json, but no dedicated Potamotrygon care page was found on the catalog. Recorded for traceability; not used for husbandry values.

additional.name: Aquarium hobby cross-check (multiple search-result sources)
additional.notes: Used together with raylady.com and Aqua Imports to triangulate tank-size, captive-adult disc width (~30-40 cm typical), and water-quality sensitivity. Cited as a representative URL from the hobby cross-check.

fish.breedingNotes: Reproduces by matrotrophic viviparity (live-bearing with direct maternal nutrient transfer). Gestation is generally around 9–12 weeks, with litters of 1–8 pups. Successful captive breeding requires specialist-level water-quality management and careful pair selection.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Adult disc width 35-50 cm requires a 2000+ L tank with very large footprint; possesses a venomous caudal spine that can inflict serious injury; possession is banned or permit-restricted in many US states and Brazilian export is tightly controlled. Suitable only for committed specialist keepers with the space, budget, water-quality discipline, and legal clearance to maintain the species for its 10-20+ year lifespan.
